comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers around the use of Starlink satellite internet on flights, with users sharing experiences, technical questions, and opinions on its impact and availability. Some highlight the benefits of free or fast inflight WiFi, while others critique the reliance on private satellite networks versus traditional infrastructure.

comments.author: neilsharma425
comments.text:
Neat problem to work on. The tail number lookup is the hard part and it sounds like you solved it the right way, by finding the people who actually track this obsessively rather than trying to scrape it yourself.

Two questions: how stale does the tail assignment data get in practice, and do you have a way to detect when an enthusiast spreadsheet goes unmaintained? And what happens to your probability estimate when an airline swaps aircraft last minute, which seems to happen pretty often on regional routes?
comments.analysis: This comment provides a knowledgeable insight into the technical challenges of tracking Starlink availability on flights and asks relevant questions about data freshness and handling last-minute aircraft swaps.

comments.author: rayiner
comments.text: I tried Starlink on a United flight the other day (short hop from Hilton Head to DC) and it was amazing.
comments.analysis: A firsthand user experience supporting the effectiveness of Starlink inflight, adding credibility to the discussion about its quality.

comments.author: Hansenq
comments.text:
Ben Thompson interviewed UA's CEO on Starlink a few months ago.

Scott said: "It took time to negotiate, because we wanted to own the consumer data, and at the beginning, Starlink did, so that was hard, and then, the other thing was I wanted to let my big competitors in the United States finish their deals with other providers and get locked in so that we would — eventually, everyone’s going to have Starlink."

Brilliant. Just brilliant. Ensured that UA would be first (of the 3 major US carriers) to Starlink and that everyone else had to wait until their existing agreements multi-year expired before switching. UA's best CEO in decades!

https://stratechery.com/2026/an-interview-with-united-ceo-sc...
comments.analysis: Provides insider information on United Airlines' strategic approach to Starlink adoption, supported by a credible external interview link.

comments.author: apitman
comments.text: I've only had it once, but inflight Starlink is a game changer. I was able to play a ranked AoE2 game over the Pacific Ocean.
comments.analysis: A personal anecdote illustrating the low latency and reliability of Starlink inflight, which supports the technology's capabilities.

comments.author: devin
comments.text: I've written it elsewhere, but: it is such a shame that the United States saw fit to run electricity _everywhere_, no matter how rural your location, but instead of do the same for rural internet we had to wait for... a private company to launch a global network of satellites. Yes, this post is about internet access while traveling 500mph, which is a different problem, but it is so messed up that people fall over themselves about Starlink for rural connectivity when it is an incredibly complex and expensive technology with huge ongoing costs that could have been solved once and for all by simply running some wires.
comments.analysis: Offers a critical societal perspective on infrastructure priorities, highlighting the complexity and cost of satellite internet compared to wired solutions.

comments.author: HorizonXP
comments.text: This is awesome! I just came back from Cancun with my family, and I was on a WestJet flight. I was taken aback by a) free Wifi and b) how fast it was to support everyone streaming YouTube even. Your tracker let me figure out that it was a WestJet flight; now I know that I have to seek out these flights from now on.
comments.analysis: Shares a positive user experience with Starlink inflight and validates the usefulness of the flight tracker tool.

comments.author: raw_anon_1111
comments.text: Starlink is good I’m sure. But it isn’t the be all end all of high speed internet on planes.  Delta doesn’t use Starlink and most of its planes have fast satellite internet except the A900s used for short hops.
comments.analysis: Provides a balanced view that other satellite internet providers also offer good inflight connectivity, tempering the Starlink hype.

comments.author: dvno42
comments.text: United has this on some flights. It's no cost but they force you watch ads in the captive portal. I'd rather pay the $8 and be left in peace, every time.
comments.analysis: Gives practical insight into the user experience and trade-offs of Starlink inflight internet on United Airlines.

comments.author: gpt5
comments.text: One nice thing about Starlink is that they force the airlines to offer it for free. I’m not sure why SpaceX is doing this, but it was surprising enough to me that my international WiFi was not only fast, but completely free that I researched it.
comments.analysis: Shares a positive impression of Starlink's free inflight WiFi but speculates on SpaceX's motives without evidence.

comments.author: nomilk
comments.text: It would be great to make this data into a browser extension that overlays the info when using Google Flights
comments.analysis: A constructive suggestion to improve user experience by integrating Starlink availability data with popular flight search tools.
